This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.

You can expect:

  • AWS walkthroughs
  • infrastructure and automation notes
  • security-focused troubleshooting
  • practical commands and setup steps

Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
title: About

content:
In AWS, we might accidentally leave Security Group rules open to:
0.0.0.0/0.
That means the resource could be reachable from anywhere on the internet. The challenge is not only finding those risky rules, but also understanding the full context in one place:
which account owns the Security Group which Security Group ID it is which EC2 instance or resource is attached to it and which Region it is running in In this project, I built a centralized architecture that can automatically detect these cases across multiple AWS accounts and export a report with all of that information in one setup.
The solution uses AWS Lambda, AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, and EC2 APIs to scan Security Groups, identify attached resources, store reports in Amazon S3, and send a summary to Slack.
When working in a multi-account AWS environment, one common security need is to review Amazon EC2 security groups across all accounts.
In this project, I built an automated solution that:
runs from a central AWS account discovers accounts in AWS Organizations scans security groups across accounts and regions detects risky ingress rules open to 0.0.0.0/0 focuses on sensitive ports such as: 22 (SSH) 3389 (RDP) 3306 (MySQL) writes the results as both: JSON report Markdown report This project helped me understand how to combine AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, Lambda, IAM, EC2, and S3 in a real-world security automation workflow.
Project Goal The goal was to scan security groups across multiple AWS accounts and identify risky ingress rules exposed to the internet.
Specifically, I wanted to detect security group rules where:
source is 0.0.0.0/0 port is one of: 22 3389 3306 I also wanted to include both:
member accounts management account Architecture The final solution works like this:
A Lambda function runs in a central AWS account. The Lambda calls AWS Organizations to list all active accounts. For each member account, Lambda uses STS AssumeRole to assume a cross-account role named SecurityAuditRole. For the management account, Lambda scans directly using its own execution role. Lambda calls EC2 APIs to retrieve security groups and their rules. Lambda filters only risky ingress rules. Lambda writes the output to Amazon S3 as: report.json report.md This is IAM Workflow that we will be doing Before You Start You need:
an AWS Organization one management account one or more member accounts permission to create: IAM roles Lambda S3 bucket EventBridge schedule a Slack workspace where you can create an Incoming Webhook Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket for Reports In the management account, create an S3 bucket `security-audit-reports
This bucket will store:
report.json report.md Step 2: Create the Lambda Execution Role in the Management Account Role name `LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole
Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "OrganizationsRead", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "organizations:ListAccounts", "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "AssumeMemberSecurityRole", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "sts:AssumeRole", "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:role/SecurityAuditRole" }, { "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents" ], "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "WriteReportsToS3", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "s3:PutObject" ], "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::security-audit-reports-254292659128-us-east-1-an/*" }, { "Sid": "Ec2ReadForManagementAccountScan", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Trust relationships
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } This role is used by the Lambda function.
Step 3: Create the Cross-Account Role in Each Member Account Now switch to each member account and create a role named:
`SecurityAuditRole
This is the role the Lambda will assume using STS.
Trust policy This trust policy allows the Lambda role in the management account to assume it.
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::MANAGEMENT-ACCOUNT-ID:role/LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "ReadEc2SecurityGroups", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Step 4: Create the Lambda Function Go back to the management account.
Create a Lambda function.
Function name: security-audit Runtime: Python 3.x Execution role: LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole Lambda Code github: <>
Step 5: Add Lambda Environment Variables and Timeout In Lambda, go to:
Configuration → Environment variables
Add these variables:
ROLE_NAME = SecurityAuditRole REPORT_BUCKET = your-s3-bucket-name REPORT_PREFIX = security-group-audit REGION_LIST = us-east-1,eu-west-1 SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL = https://hooks.slack.com/services/xxxx/yyyy/zzzz SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS = false
What they mean ROLE_NAME
Role name to assume in member accounts REPORT_BUCKET
S3 bucket name REPORT_PREFIX
Folder-like prefix inside S3 REGION_LIST
Regions to scan SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
Slack Incoming Webhook URL SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS
Whether to send Slack even when no findings exist Increase Timeout Increae timeout to 1min 30 sec because we have to scan all the member account so it need to wait time. You can adjust as you like.
Step 6: Create a Slack Incoming Webhook In Slack:
create or choose a channel create an Incoming Webhook copy the webhook URL paste it into the Lambda env variable SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL This allows Lambda to send a summary message to Slack after every run.
Step 7: Deploy and Test the Lambda Manually Create a simple Lambda test event: `{}
Then click Test.
After the run, check these places:
Lambda response CloudWatch Logs S3 bucket Slack channel You should see:
report.json in S3 report.md in S3 Slack summary message These are my security groups that open 0.0.0.0/0 to 3306 and 80. You can also see the ec2 instance name.
Step 8: Add EventBridge Scheduler Right now, manual testing works.= Now automate it.
Go to: Amazon EventBridge -> Schedules → Create schedule
Settings Name: security-audit-daily
Schedule pattern:
rate(1 day)
Target:
Lambda function choose security-audit Execution role for schedule Choose:
Create new role for this schedule This role only needs to invoke Lambda.
Step 9: Verify Automatic Execution Wait for the schedule to trigger the Lambda. ( You can test with 3 min Recurring schdule )
Then verify:
Lambda ran automatically new S3 reports were created Slack received a summary message Final Result At the end of this project, I had a solution that:
scans both management and member accounts detects risky ingress rules focuses only on sensitive ports shows attached resources writes JSON and Markdown reports to S3 sends Slack notifications runs automatically on a schedule Final Thoughts This was one of the best projects I built for learning AWS in a practical way.
It touched a lot of real AWS concepts:
Organizations STS AssumeRole IAM trust and permission policies Lambda execution roles EC2 APIs S3 reporting EventBridge automation Slack integration Most importantly, it helped me move from:
“I know the services”
to
“I can connect them together to solve a real problem”
That is where AWS learning starts to feel real.
summary: Build a centralized AWS security audit workflow that finds risky internet-exposed security group rules across member and management accounts.
title: How to Track Down Risky Security Group Rules Across All of Your AWS Accounts using Lambda

content:
This note demonstrates how to set up a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS, expose it externally, and secure it with HTTPS using AWS Certificate Manager and Route 53.
Prerequisites AWS CLI configured with appropriate permissions eksctl installed kubectl installed and configured Step 1: Create an EKS Cluster First, create a test EKS cluster using eksctl:
eksctl create cluster \ --name test-eks \ --region ap-south-1 \ --version 1.35 \ --instance-types t3.small \ --nodes 1 \ --nodes-min 1 \ --nodes-max 1 This command creates a single-node EKS cluster in the ap-south-1 region.
Step 2: Deploy Sample Applications We’ll deploy two sample applications: a counting service and a dashboard that consumes it.
Counting Service Create counting.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: counting --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: counting spec: ports: - port: 9001 targetPort: 9001 selector: app: counting --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: counting spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: counting template: metadata: labels: app: counting spec: containers: - name: counting image: hashicorp/counting-service:0.0.2 ports: - containerPort: 9001 Apply it:
kubectl apply -f counting.yaml Dashboard Service Create dashboard.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: dashboard --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: labels: app: dashboard name: dashboard spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: dashboard template: metadata: annotations: labels: app: dashboard spec: serviceAccountName: dashboard containers: - name: dashboard image: hashicorp/dashboard-service:0.0.4 ports: - containerPort: 9002 env: - name: COUNTING_SERVICE_URL value: 'http://counting.default.svc.cluster.local:9001' Apply it:
kubectl apply -f dashboard.yaml Step 3: Verify Services Check the services:
kubectl get svc Output:
NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE counting ClusterIP 10.100.149.59 <none> 9001/TCP 7m25s dashboard LoadBalancer 10.100.89.242 a9dd99b117d0c4a35b11a12334f42ac6-409196634.ap-south-1.elb.amazonaws.com 80:30582/TCP 7m24s kubernetes ClusterIP 10.100.0.1 <none> 443/TCP 26m The dashboard service has an external IP provided by AWS Classic Load Balancer.
Step 4: Access via HTTP Initially, the service is accessible via HTTP on port 80.
![[Pasted Image 20260427093556_127.png]]
Step 5: Add DNS with Route 53 Create a CNAME record in Route 53 pointing to the Load Balancer DNS name.
Domain: dashboard.learnops.site
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.webp]]
Step 6: Configure HTTPS Add SSL Certificate Create a public certificate in AWS Certificate Manager for your domain.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-3.webp]]
Update Load Balancer Listener Modify the Load Balancer to use HTTPS on port 443, attaching the certificate.
Before editing:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.webp]]
After adding HTTPS:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-4.webp]]
Add lobalancer sg to 443 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-6.webp]]
The application is now securely accessible via HTTPS at https://dashboard.learnops.site.
Troubleshooting After configuring HTTPS, you may encounter issues where the dashboard can access the external URL, but the counting service cannot be accessed anymore. This might manifest as a WebSocket error.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-7.webp]]
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-8.webp]]
ok now ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-9.webp]]
But change back to this ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.png]]
There is websocket error.
Cloud controller manager do when we change service to loadbalancer. it apply to classic load balancer.
ccm is in eks control plane node but we can’t see it
https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/architecture/cloud-controller/#functions-of-the-ccm
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-1.png]]
Now we will use with NLB
https://cloud-provider-aws.sigs.k8s.io/service_controller/ you can see in this link about service controller
apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard annotations: service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.png]]
Architecture Diagram ![[2026-04-27 09.28.excalidraw]]
Cleanup To clean up:
eksctl delete cluster --name test-eks --region ap-south-1 This removes the EKS cluster and associated resources.
summary: Build a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS.
title: Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS

content:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.
You can expect:
AWS walkthroughs infrastructure and automation notes security-focused troubleshooting practical commands and setup steps Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
summary:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.

You can expect:

  • AWS walkthroughs
  • infrastructure and automation notes
  • security-focused troubleshooting
  • practical commands and setup steps

Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
title: About

content:
In AWS, we might accidentally leave Security Group rules open to:
0.0.0.0/0.
That means the resource could be reachable from anywhere on the internet. The challenge is not only finding those risky rules, but also understanding the full context in one place:
which account owns the Security Group which Security Group ID it is which EC2 instance or resource is attached to it and which Region it is running in In this project, I built a centralized architecture that can automatically detect these cases across multiple AWS accounts and export a report with all of that information in one setup.
The solution uses AWS Lambda, AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, and EC2 APIs to scan Security Groups, identify attached resources, store reports in Amazon S3, and send a summary to Slack.
When working in a multi-account AWS environment, one common security need is to review Amazon EC2 security groups across all accounts.
In this project, I built an automated solution that:
runs from a central AWS account discovers accounts in AWS Organizations scans security groups across accounts and regions detects risky ingress rules open to 0.0.0.0/0 focuses on sensitive ports such as: 22 (SSH) 3389 (RDP) 3306 (MySQL) writes the results as both: JSON report Markdown report This project helped me understand how to combine AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, Lambda, IAM, EC2, and S3 in a real-world security automation workflow.
Project Goal The goal was to scan security groups across multiple AWS accounts and identify risky ingress rules exposed to the internet.
Specifically, I wanted to detect security group rules where:
source is 0.0.0.0/0 port is one of: 22 3389 3306 I also wanted to include both:
member accounts management account Architecture The final solution works like this:
A Lambda function runs in a central AWS account. The Lambda calls AWS Organizations to list all active accounts. For each member account, Lambda uses STS AssumeRole to assume a cross-account role named SecurityAuditRole. For the management account, Lambda scans directly using its own execution role. Lambda calls EC2 APIs to retrieve security groups and their rules. Lambda filters only risky ingress rules. Lambda writes the output to Amazon S3 as: report.json report.md This is IAM Workflow that we will be doing Before You Start You need:
an AWS Organization one management account one or more member accounts permission to create: IAM roles Lambda S3 bucket EventBridge schedule a Slack workspace where you can create an Incoming Webhook Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket for Reports In the management account, create an S3 bucket `security-audit-reports
This bucket will store:
report.json report.md Step 2: Create the Lambda Execution Role in the Management Account Role name `LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole
Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "OrganizationsRead", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "organizations:ListAccounts", "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "AssumeMemberSecurityRole", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "sts:AssumeRole", "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:role/SecurityAuditRole" }, { "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents" ], "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "WriteReportsToS3", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "s3:PutObject" ], "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::security-audit-reports-254292659128-us-east-1-an/*" }, { "Sid": "Ec2ReadForManagementAccountScan", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Trust relationships
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } This role is used by the Lambda function.
Step 3: Create the Cross-Account Role in Each Member Account Now switch to each member account and create a role named:
`SecurityAuditRole
This is the role the Lambda will assume using STS.
Trust policy This trust policy allows the Lambda role in the management account to assume it.
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::MANAGEMENT-ACCOUNT-ID:role/LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "ReadEc2SecurityGroups", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Step 4: Create the Lambda Function Go back to the management account.
Create a Lambda function.
Function name: security-audit Runtime: Python 3.x Execution role: LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole Lambda Code github: <>
Step 5: Add Lambda Environment Variables and Timeout In Lambda, go to:
Configuration → Environment variables
Add these variables:
ROLE_NAME = SecurityAuditRole REPORT_BUCKET = your-s3-bucket-name REPORT_PREFIX = security-group-audit REGION_LIST = us-east-1,eu-west-1 SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL = https://hooks.slack.com/services/xxxx/yyyy/zzzz SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS = false
What they mean ROLE_NAME
Role name to assume in member accounts REPORT_BUCKET
S3 bucket name REPORT_PREFIX
Folder-like prefix inside S3 REGION_LIST
Regions to scan SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
Slack Incoming Webhook URL SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS
Whether to send Slack even when no findings exist Increase Timeout Increae timeout to 1min 30 sec because we have to scan all the member account so it need to wait time. You can adjust as you like.
Step 6: Create a Slack Incoming Webhook In Slack:
create or choose a channel create an Incoming Webhook copy the webhook URL paste it into the Lambda env variable SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL This allows Lambda to send a summary message to Slack after every run.
Step 7: Deploy and Test the Lambda Manually Create a simple Lambda test event: `{}
Then click Test.
After the run, check these places:
Lambda response CloudWatch Logs S3 bucket Slack channel You should see:
report.json in S3 report.md in S3 Slack summary message These are my security groups that open 0.0.0.0/0 to 3306 and 80. You can also see the ec2 instance name.
Step 8: Add EventBridge Scheduler Right now, manual testing works.= Now automate it.
Go to: Amazon EventBridge -> Schedules → Create schedule
Settings Name: security-audit-daily
Schedule pattern:
rate(1 day)
Target:
Lambda function choose security-audit Execution role for schedule Choose:
Create new role for this schedule This role only needs to invoke Lambda.
Step 9: Verify Automatic Execution Wait for the schedule to trigger the Lambda. ( You can test with 3 min Recurring schdule )
Then verify:
Lambda ran automatically new S3 reports were created Slack received a summary message Final Result At the end of this project, I had a solution that:
scans both management and member accounts detects risky ingress rules focuses only on sensitive ports shows attached resources writes JSON and Markdown reports to S3 sends Slack notifications runs automatically on a schedule Final Thoughts This was one of the best projects I built for learning AWS in a practical way.
It touched a lot of real AWS concepts:
Organizations STS AssumeRole IAM trust and permission policies Lambda execution roles EC2 APIs S3 reporting EventBridge automation Slack integration Most importantly, it helped me move from:
“I know the services”
to
“I can connect them together to solve a real problem”
That is where AWS learning starts to feel real.
summary: Build a centralized AWS security audit workflow that finds risky internet-exposed security group rules across member and management accounts.
title: How to Track Down Risky Security Group Rules Across All of Your AWS Accounts using Lambda

content:
This note demonstrates how to set up a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS, expose it externally, and secure it with HTTPS using AWS Certificate Manager and Route 53.
Prerequisites AWS CLI configured with appropriate permissions eksctl installed kubectl installed and configured Step 1: Create an EKS Cluster First, create a test EKS cluster using eksctl:
eksctl create cluster \ --name test-eks \ --region ap-south-1 \ --version 1.35 \ --instance-types t3.small \ --nodes 1 \ --nodes-min 1 \ --nodes-max 1 This command creates a single-node EKS cluster in the ap-south-1 region.
Step 2: Deploy Sample Applications We’ll deploy two sample applications: a counting service and a dashboard that consumes it.
Counting Service Create counting.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: counting --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: counting spec: ports: - port: 9001 targetPort: 9001 selector: app: counting --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: counting spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: counting template: metadata: labels: app: counting spec: containers: - name: counting image: hashicorp/counting-service:0.0.2 ports: - containerPort: 9001 Apply it:
kubectl apply -f counting.yaml Dashboard Service Create dashboard.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: dashboard --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: labels: app: dashboard name: dashboard spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: dashboard template: metadata: annotations: labels: app: dashboard spec: serviceAccountName: dashboard containers: - name: dashboard image: hashicorp/dashboard-service:0.0.4 ports: - containerPort: 9002 env: - name: COUNTING_SERVICE_URL value: 'http://counting.default.svc.cluster.local:9001' Apply it:
kubectl apply -f dashboard.yaml Step 3: Verify Services Check the services:
kubectl get svc Output:
NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE counting ClusterIP 10.100.149.59 <none> 9001/TCP 7m25s dashboard LoadBalancer 10.100.89.242 a9dd99b117d0c4a35b11a12334f42ac6-409196634.ap-south-1.elb.amazonaws.com 80:30582/TCP 7m24s kubernetes ClusterIP 10.100.0.1 <none> 443/TCP 26m The dashboard service has an external IP provided by AWS Classic Load Balancer.
Step 4: Access via HTTP Initially, the service is accessible via HTTP on port 80.
![[Pasted Image 20260427093556_127.png]]
Step 5: Add DNS with Route 53 Create a CNAME record in Route 53 pointing to the Load Balancer DNS name.
Domain: dashboard.learnops.site
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.webp]]
Step 6: Configure HTTPS Add SSL Certificate Create a public certificate in AWS Certificate Manager for your domain.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-3.webp]]
Update Load Balancer Listener Modify the Load Balancer to use HTTPS on port 443, attaching the certificate.
Before editing:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.webp]]
After adding HTTPS:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-4.webp]]
Add lobalancer sg to 443 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-6.webp]]
The application is now securely accessible via HTTPS at https://dashboard.learnops.site.
Troubleshooting After configuring HTTPS, you may encounter issues where the dashboard can access the external URL, but the counting service cannot be accessed anymore. This might manifest as a WebSocket error.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-7.webp]]
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-8.webp]]
ok now ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-9.webp]]
But change back to this ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.png]]
There is websocket error.
Cloud controller manager do when we change service to loadbalancer. it apply to classic load balancer.
ccm is in eks control plane node but we can’t see it
https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/architecture/cloud-controller/#functions-of-the-ccm
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-1.png]]
Now we will use with NLB
https://cloud-provider-aws.sigs.k8s.io/service_controller/ you can see in this link about service controller
apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard annotations: service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.png]]
Architecture Diagram ![[2026-04-27 09.28.excalidraw]]
Cleanup To clean up:
eksctl delete cluster --name test-eks --region ap-south-1 This removes the EKS cluster and associated resources.
summary: Build a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS.
title: Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS

content:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.
You can expect:
AWS walkthroughs infrastructure and automation notes security-focused troubleshooting practical commands and setup steps Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
summary:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.

You can expect:

  • AWS walkthroughs
  • infrastructure and automation notes
  • security-focused troubleshooting
  • practical commands and setup steps

Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
title: About

content:
In AWS, we might accidentally leave Security Group rules open to:
0.0.0.0/0.
That means the resource could be reachable from anywhere on the internet. The challenge is not only finding those risky rules, but also understanding the full context in one place:
which account owns the Security Group which Security Group ID it is which EC2 instance or resource is attached to it and which Region it is running in In this project, I built a centralized architecture that can automatically detect these cases across multiple AWS accounts and export a report with all of that information in one setup.
The solution uses AWS Lambda, AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, and EC2 APIs to scan Security Groups, identify attached resources, store reports in Amazon S3, and send a summary to Slack.
When working in a multi-account AWS environment, one common security need is to review Amazon EC2 security groups across all accounts.
In this project, I built an automated solution that:
runs from a central AWS account discovers accounts in AWS Organizations scans security groups across accounts and regions detects risky ingress rules open to 0.0.0.0/0 focuses on sensitive ports such as: 22 (SSH) 3389 (RDP) 3306 (MySQL) writes the results as both: JSON report Markdown report This project helped me understand how to combine AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, Lambda, IAM, EC2, and S3 in a real-world security automation workflow.
Project Goal The goal was to scan security groups across multiple AWS accounts and identify risky ingress rules exposed to the internet.
Specifically, I wanted to detect security group rules where:
source is 0.0.0.0/0 port is one of: 22 3389 3306 I also wanted to include both:
member accounts management account Architecture The final solution works like this:
A Lambda function runs in a central AWS account. The Lambda calls AWS Organizations to list all active accounts. For each member account, Lambda uses STS AssumeRole to assume a cross-account role named SecurityAuditRole. For the management account, Lambda scans directly using its own execution role. Lambda calls EC2 APIs to retrieve security groups and their rules. Lambda filters only risky ingress rules. Lambda writes the output to Amazon S3 as: report.json report.md This is IAM Workflow that we will be doing Before You Start You need:
an AWS Organization one management account one or more member accounts permission to create: IAM roles Lambda S3 bucket EventBridge schedule a Slack workspace where you can create an Incoming Webhook Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket for Reports In the management account, create an S3 bucket `security-audit-reports
This bucket will store:
report.json report.md Step 2: Create the Lambda Execution Role in the Management Account Role name `LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole
Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "OrganizationsRead", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "organizations:ListAccounts", "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "AssumeMemberSecurityRole", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "sts:AssumeRole", "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:role/SecurityAuditRole" }, { "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents" ], "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "WriteReportsToS3", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "s3:PutObject" ], "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::security-audit-reports-254292659128-us-east-1-an/*" }, { "Sid": "Ec2ReadForManagementAccountScan", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Trust relationships
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } This role is used by the Lambda function.
Step 3: Create the Cross-Account Role in Each Member Account Now switch to each member account and create a role named:
`SecurityAuditRole
This is the role the Lambda will assume using STS.
Trust policy This trust policy allows the Lambda role in the management account to assume it.
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::MANAGEMENT-ACCOUNT-ID:role/LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "ReadEc2SecurityGroups", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Step 4: Create the Lambda Function Go back to the management account.
Create a Lambda function.
Function name: security-audit Runtime: Python 3.x Execution role: LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole Lambda Code github: <>
Step 5: Add Lambda Environment Variables and Timeout In Lambda, go to:
Configuration → Environment variables
Add these variables:
ROLE_NAME = SecurityAuditRole REPORT_BUCKET = your-s3-bucket-name REPORT_PREFIX = security-group-audit REGION_LIST = us-east-1,eu-west-1 SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL = https://hooks.slack.com/services/xxxx/yyyy/zzzz SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS = false
What they mean ROLE_NAME
Role name to assume in member accounts REPORT_BUCKET
S3 bucket name REPORT_PREFIX
Folder-like prefix inside S3 REGION_LIST
Regions to scan SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
Slack Incoming Webhook URL SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS
Whether to send Slack even when no findings exist Increase Timeout Increae timeout to 1min 30 sec because we have to scan all the member account so it need to wait time. You can adjust as you like.
Step 6: Create a Slack Incoming Webhook In Slack:
create or choose a channel create an Incoming Webhook copy the webhook URL paste it into the Lambda env variable SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL This allows Lambda to send a summary message to Slack after every run.
Step 7: Deploy and Test the Lambda Manually Create a simple Lambda test event: `{}
Then click Test.
After the run, check these places:
Lambda response CloudWatch Logs S3 bucket Slack channel You should see:
report.json in S3 report.md in S3 Slack summary message These are my security groups that open 0.0.0.0/0 to 3306 and 80. You can also see the ec2 instance name.
Step 8: Add EventBridge Scheduler Right now, manual testing works.= Now automate it.
Go to: Amazon EventBridge -> Schedules → Create schedule
Settings Name: security-audit-daily
Schedule pattern:
rate(1 day)
Target:
Lambda function choose security-audit Execution role for schedule Choose:
Create new role for this schedule This role only needs to invoke Lambda.
Step 9: Verify Automatic Execution Wait for the schedule to trigger the Lambda. ( You can test with 3 min Recurring schdule )
Then verify:
Lambda ran automatically new S3 reports were created Slack received a summary message Final Result At the end of this project, I had a solution that:
scans both management and member accounts detects risky ingress rules focuses only on sensitive ports shows attached resources writes JSON and Markdown reports to S3 sends Slack notifications runs automatically on a schedule Final Thoughts This was one of the best projects I built for learning AWS in a practical way.
It touched a lot of real AWS concepts:
Organizations STS AssumeRole IAM trust and permission policies Lambda execution roles EC2 APIs S3 reporting EventBridge automation Slack integration Most importantly, it helped me move from:
“I know the services”
to
“I can connect them together to solve a real problem”
That is where AWS learning starts to feel real.
summary: Build a centralized AWS security audit workflow that finds risky internet-exposed security group rules across member and management accounts.
title: How to Track Down Risky Security Group Rules Across All of Your AWS Accounts using Lambda

content:
This note demonstrates how to set up a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS, expose it externally, and secure it with HTTPS using AWS Certificate Manager and Route 53.
Prerequisites AWS CLI configured with appropriate permissions eksctl installed kubectl installed and configured Step 1: Create an EKS Cluster First, create a test EKS cluster using eksctl:
eksctl create cluster \ --name test-eks \ --region ap-south-1 \ --version 1.35 \ --instance-types t3.small \ --nodes 1 \ --nodes-min 1 \ --nodes-max 1 This command creates a single-node EKS cluster in the ap-south-1 region.
Step 2: Deploy Sample Applications We’ll deploy two sample applications: a counting service and a dashboard that consumes it.
Counting Service Create counting.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: counting --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: counting spec: ports: - port: 9001 targetPort: 9001 selector: app: counting --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: counting spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: counting template: metadata: labels: app: counting spec: containers: - name: counting image: hashicorp/counting-service:0.0.2 ports: - containerPort: 9001 Apply it:
kubectl apply -f counting.yaml Dashboard Service Create dashboard.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: dashboard --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: labels: app: dashboard name: dashboard spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: dashboard template: metadata: annotations: labels: app: dashboard spec: serviceAccountName: dashboard containers: - name: dashboard image: hashicorp/dashboard-service:0.0.4 ports: - containerPort: 9002 env: - name: COUNTING_SERVICE_URL value: 'http://counting.default.svc.cluster.local:9001' Apply it:
kubectl apply -f dashboard.yaml Step 3: Verify Services Check the services:
kubectl get svc Output:
NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE counting ClusterIP 10.100.149.59 <none> 9001/TCP 7m25s dashboard LoadBalancer 10.100.89.242 a9dd99b117d0c4a35b11a12334f42ac6-409196634.ap-south-1.elb.amazonaws.com 80:30582/TCP 7m24s kubernetes ClusterIP 10.100.0.1 <none> 443/TCP 26m The dashboard service has an external IP provided by AWS Classic Load Balancer.
Step 4: Access via HTTP Initially, the service is accessible via HTTP on port 80.
![[Pasted Image 20260427093556_127.png]]
Step 5: Add DNS with Route 53 Create a CNAME record in Route 53 pointing to the Load Balancer DNS name.
Domain: dashboard.learnops.site
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.webp]]
Step 6: Configure HTTPS Add SSL Certificate Create a public certificate in AWS Certificate Manager for your domain.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-3.webp]]
Update Load Balancer Listener Modify the Load Balancer to use HTTPS on port 443, attaching the certificate.
Before editing:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.webp]]
After adding HTTPS:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-4.webp]]
Add lobalancer sg to 443 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-6.webp]]
The application is now securely accessible via HTTPS at https://dashboard.learnops.site.
Troubleshooting After configuring HTTPS, you may encounter issues where the dashboard can access the external URL, but the counting service cannot be accessed anymore. This might manifest as a WebSocket error.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-7.webp]]
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-8.webp]]
ok now ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-9.webp]]
But change back to this ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.png]]
There is websocket error.
Cloud controller manager do when we change service to loadbalancer. it apply to classic load balancer.
ccm is in eks control plane node but we can’t see it
https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/architecture/cloud-controller/#functions-of-the-ccm
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-1.png]]
Now we will use with NLB
https://cloud-provider-aws.sigs.k8s.io/service_controller/ you can see in this link about service controller
apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard annotations: service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.png]]
Architecture Diagram Cleanup To clean up:
eksctl delete cluster --name test-eks --region ap-south-1 This removes the EKS cluster and associated resources.
summary: Build a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS.
title: Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS

content:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.
You can expect:
AWS walkthroughs infrastructure and automation notes security-focused troubleshooting practical commands and setup steps Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
summary:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.

You can expect:

  • AWS walkthroughs
  • infrastructure and automation notes
  • security-focused troubleshooting
  • practical commands and setup steps

Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
title: About

content:
In AWS, we might accidentally leave Security Group rules open to:
0.0.0.0/0.
That means the resource could be reachable from anywhere on the internet. The challenge is not only finding those risky rules, but also understanding the full context in one place:
which account owns the Security Group which Security Group ID it is which EC2 instance or resource is attached to it and which Region it is running in In this project, I built a centralized architecture that can automatically detect these cases across multiple AWS accounts and export a report with all of that information in one setup.
The solution uses AWS Lambda, AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, and EC2 APIs to scan Security Groups, identify attached resources, store reports in Amazon S3, and send a summary to Slack.
When working in a multi-account AWS environment, one common security need is to review Amazon EC2 security groups across all accounts.
In this project, I built an automated solution that:
runs from a central AWS account discovers accounts in AWS Organizations scans security groups across accounts and regions detects risky ingress rules open to 0.0.0.0/0 focuses on sensitive ports such as: 22 (SSH) 3389 (RDP) 3306 (MySQL) writes the results as both: JSON report Markdown report This project helped me understand how to combine AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, Lambda, IAM, EC2, and S3 in a real-world security automation workflow.
Project Goal The goal was to scan security groups across multiple AWS accounts and identify risky ingress rules exposed to the internet.
Specifically, I wanted to detect security group rules where:
source is 0.0.0.0/0 port is one of: 22 3389 3306 I also wanted to include both:
member accounts management account Architecture The final solution works like this:
A Lambda function runs in a central AWS account. The Lambda calls AWS Organizations to list all active accounts. For each member account, Lambda uses STS AssumeRole to assume a cross-account role named SecurityAuditRole. For the management account, Lambda scans directly using its own execution role. Lambda calls EC2 APIs to retrieve security groups and their rules. Lambda filters only risky ingress rules. Lambda writes the output to Amazon S3 as: report.json report.md This is IAM Workflow that we will be doing Before You Start You need:
an AWS Organization one management account one or more member accounts permission to create: IAM roles Lambda S3 bucket EventBridge schedule a Slack workspace where you can create an Incoming Webhook Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket for Reports In the management account, create an S3 bucket `security-audit-reports
This bucket will store:
report.json report.md Step 2: Create the Lambda Execution Role in the Management Account Role name `LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole
Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "OrganizationsRead", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "organizations:ListAccounts", "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "AssumeMemberSecurityRole", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "sts:AssumeRole", "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:role/SecurityAuditRole" }, { "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents" ], "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "WriteReportsToS3", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "s3:PutObject" ], "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::security-audit-reports-254292659128-us-east-1-an/*" }, { "Sid": "Ec2ReadForManagementAccountScan", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Trust relationships
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } This role is used by the Lambda function.
Step 3: Create the Cross-Account Role in Each Member Account Now switch to each member account and create a role named:
`SecurityAuditRole
This is the role the Lambda will assume using STS.
Trust policy This trust policy allows the Lambda role in the management account to assume it.
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::MANAGEMENT-ACCOUNT-ID:role/LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "ReadEc2SecurityGroups", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Step 4: Create the Lambda Function Go back to the management account.
Create a Lambda function.
Function name: security-audit Runtime: Python 3.x Execution role: LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole Lambda Code github: <>
Step 5: Add Lambda Environment Variables and Timeout In Lambda, go to:
Configuration → Environment variables
Add these variables:
ROLE_NAME = SecurityAuditRole REPORT_BUCKET = your-s3-bucket-name REPORT_PREFIX = security-group-audit REGION_LIST = us-east-1,eu-west-1 SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL = https://hooks.slack.com/services/xxxx/yyyy/zzzz SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS = false
What they mean ROLE_NAME
Role name to assume in member accounts REPORT_BUCKET
S3 bucket name REPORT_PREFIX
Folder-like prefix inside S3 REGION_LIST
Regions to scan SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
Slack Incoming Webhook URL SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS
Whether to send Slack even when no findings exist Increase Timeout Increae timeout to 1min 30 sec because we have to scan all the member account so it need to wait time. You can adjust as you like.
Step 6: Create a Slack Incoming Webhook In Slack:
create or choose a channel create an Incoming Webhook copy the webhook URL paste it into the Lambda env variable SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL This allows Lambda to send a summary message to Slack after every run.
Step 7: Deploy and Test the Lambda Manually Create a simple Lambda test event: `{}
Then click Test.
After the run, check these places:
Lambda response CloudWatch Logs S3 bucket Slack channel You should see:
report.json in S3 report.md in S3 Slack summary message These are my security groups that open 0.0.0.0/0 to 3306 and 80. You can also see the ec2 instance name.
Step 8: Add EventBridge Scheduler Right now, manual testing works.= Now automate it.
Go to: Amazon EventBridge -> Schedules → Create schedule
Settings Name: security-audit-daily
Schedule pattern:
rate(1 day)
Target:
Lambda function choose security-audit Execution role for schedule Choose:
Create new role for this schedule This role only needs to invoke Lambda.
Step 9: Verify Automatic Execution Wait for the schedule to trigger the Lambda. ( You can test with 3 min Recurring schdule )
Then verify:
Lambda ran automatically new S3 reports were created Slack received a summary message Final Result At the end of this project, I had a solution that:
scans both management and member accounts detects risky ingress rules focuses only on sensitive ports shows attached resources writes JSON and Markdown reports to S3 sends Slack notifications runs automatically on a schedule Final Thoughts This was one of the best projects I built for learning AWS in a practical way.
It touched a lot of real AWS concepts:
Organizations STS AssumeRole IAM trust and permission policies Lambda execution roles EC2 APIs S3 reporting EventBridge automation Slack integration Most importantly, it helped me move from:
“I know the services”
to
“I can connect them together to solve a real problem”
That is where AWS learning starts to feel real.
summary: Build a centralized AWS security audit workflow that finds risky internet-exposed security group rules across member and management accounts.
title: How to Track Down Risky Security Group Rules Across All of Your AWS Accounts using Lambda

content:
This note demonstrates how to set up a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS, expose it externally, and secure it with HTTPS using AWS Certificate Manager and Route 53.
Prerequisites AWS CLI configured with appropriate permissions eksctl installed kubectl installed and configured Step 1: Create an EKS Cluster First, create a test EKS cluster using eksctl:
eksctl create cluster \ --name test-eks \ --region ap-south-1 \ --version 1.35 \ --instance-types t3.small \ --nodes 1 \ --nodes-min 1 \ --nodes-max 1 This command creates a single-node EKS cluster in the ap-south-1 region.
Step 2: Deploy Sample Applications We’ll deploy two sample applications: a counting service and a dashboard that consumes it.
Counting Service Create counting.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: counting --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: counting spec: ports: - port: 9001 targetPort: 9001 selector: app: counting --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: counting spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: counting template: metadata: labels: app: counting spec: containers: - name: counting image: hashicorp/counting-service:0.0.2 ports: - containerPort: 9001 Apply it:
kubectl apply -f counting.yaml Dashboard Service Create dashboard.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: dashboard --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: labels: app: dashboard name: dashboard spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: dashboard template: metadata: annotations: labels: app: dashboard spec: serviceAccountName: dashboard containers: - name: dashboard image: hashicorp/dashboard-service:0.0.4 ports: - containerPort: 9002 env: - name: COUNTING_SERVICE_URL value: 'http://counting.default.svc.cluster.local:9001' Apply it:
kubectl apply -f dashboard.yaml Step 3: Verify Services Check the services:
kubectl get svc Output:
NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE counting ClusterIP 10.100.149.59 <none> 9001/TCP 7m25s dashboard LoadBalancer 10.100.89.242 a9dd99b117d0c4a35b11a12334f42ac6-409196634.ap-south-1.elb.amazonaws.com 80:30582/TCP 7m24s kubernetes ClusterIP 10.100.0.1 <none> 443/TCP 26m The dashboard service has an external IP provided by AWS Classic Load Balancer.
Step 4: Access via HTTP Initially, the service is accessible via HTTP on port 80.
![[Pasted Image 20260427093556_127.png]]
Step 5: Add DNS with Route 53 Create a CNAME record in Route 53 pointing to the Load Balancer DNS name.
Domain: dashboard.learnops.site
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.webp]]
Step 6: Configure HTTPS Add SSL Certificate Create a public certificate in AWS Certificate Manager for your domain.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-3.webp]]
Update Load Balancer Listener Modify the Load Balancer to use HTTPS on port 443, attaching the certificate.
Before editing:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.webp]]
After adding HTTPS:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-4.webp]]
Add lobalancer sg to 443 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-6.webp]]
The application is now securely accessible via HTTPS at https://dashboard.learnops.site.
Troubleshooting After configuring HTTPS, you may encounter issues where the dashboard can access the external URL, but the counting service cannot be accessed anymore. This might manifest as a WebSocket error.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-7.webp]]
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-8.webp]]
ok now ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-9.webp]]
But change back to this ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.png]]
There is websocket error.
Cloud controller manager do when we change service to loadbalancer. it apply to classic load balancer.
ccm is in eks control plane node but we can’t see it
https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/architecture/cloud-controller/#functions-of-the-ccm
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-1.png]]
Now we will use with NLB
https://cloud-provider-aws.sigs.k8s.io/service_controller/ you can see in this link about service controller
apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard annotations: service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.png]]
Architecture Diagram Cleanup To clean up:
eksctl delete cluster --name test-eks --region ap-south-1 This removes the EKS cluster and associated resources.
summary: Build a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS.
title: Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS

content:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.
You can expect:
AWS walkthroughs infrastructure and automation notes security-focused troubleshooting practical commands and setup steps Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
summary:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.

You can expect:

  • AWS walkthroughs
  • infrastructure and automation notes
  • security-focused troubleshooting
  • practical commands and setup steps

Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
title: About

content:
In AWS, we might accidentally leave Security Group rules open to:
0.0.0.0/0.
That means the resource could be reachable from anywhere on the internet. The challenge is not only finding those risky rules, but also understanding the full context in one place:
which account owns the Security Group which Security Group ID it is which EC2 instance or resource is attached to it and which Region it is running in In this project, I built a centralized architecture that can automatically detect these cases across multiple AWS accounts and export a report with all of that information in one setup.
The solution uses AWS Lambda, AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, and EC2 APIs to scan Security Groups, identify attached resources, store reports in Amazon S3, and send a summary to Slack.
When working in a multi-account AWS environment, one common security need is to review Amazon EC2 security groups across all accounts.
In this project, I built an automated solution that:
runs from a central AWS account discovers accounts in AWS Organizations scans security groups across accounts and regions detects risky ingress rules open to 0.0.0.0/0 focuses on sensitive ports such as: 22 (SSH) 3389 (RDP) 3306 (MySQL) writes the results as both: JSON report Markdown report This project helped me understand how to combine AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, Lambda, IAM, EC2, and S3 in a real-world security automation workflow.
Project Goal The goal was to scan security groups across multiple AWS accounts and identify risky ingress rules exposed to the internet.
Specifically, I wanted to detect security group rules where:
source is 0.0.0.0/0 port is one of: 22 3389 3306 I also wanted to include both:
member accounts management account Architecture The final solution works like this:
A Lambda function runs in a central AWS account. The Lambda calls AWS Organizations to list all active accounts. For each member account, Lambda uses STS AssumeRole to assume a cross-account role named SecurityAuditRole. For the management account, Lambda scans directly using its own execution role. Lambda calls EC2 APIs to retrieve security groups and their rules. Lambda filters only risky ingress rules. Lambda writes the output to Amazon S3 as: report.json report.md This is IAM Workflow that we will be doing Before You Start You need:
an AWS Organization one management account one or more member accounts permission to create: IAM roles Lambda S3 bucket EventBridge schedule a Slack workspace where you can create an Incoming Webhook Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket for Reports In the management account, create an S3 bucket `security-audit-reports
This bucket will store:
report.json report.md Step 2: Create the Lambda Execution Role in the Management Account Role name `LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole
Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "OrganizationsRead", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "organizations:ListAccounts", "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "AssumeMemberSecurityRole", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "sts:AssumeRole", "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:role/SecurityAuditRole" }, { "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents" ], "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "WriteReportsToS3", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "s3:PutObject" ], "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::security-audit-reports-254292659128-us-east-1-an/*" }, { "Sid": "Ec2ReadForManagementAccountScan", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Trust relationships
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } This role is used by the Lambda function.
Step 3: Create the Cross-Account Role in Each Member Account Now switch to each member account and create a role named:
`SecurityAuditRole
This is the role the Lambda will assume using STS.
Trust policy This trust policy allows the Lambda role in the management account to assume it.
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::MANAGEMENT-ACCOUNT-ID:role/LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "ReadEc2SecurityGroups", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Step 4: Create the Lambda Function Go back to the management account.
Create a Lambda function.
Function name: security-audit Runtime: Python 3.x Execution role: LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole Lambda Code github: <>
Step 5: Add Lambda Environment Variables and Timeout In Lambda, go to:
Configuration → Environment variables
Add these variables:
ROLE_NAME = SecurityAuditRole REPORT_BUCKET = your-s3-bucket-name REPORT_PREFIX = security-group-audit REGION_LIST = us-east-1,eu-west-1 SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL = https://hooks.slack.com/services/xxxx/yyyy/zzzz SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS = false
What they mean ROLE_NAME
Role name to assume in member accounts REPORT_BUCKET
S3 bucket name REPORT_PREFIX
Folder-like prefix inside S3 REGION_LIST
Regions to scan SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
Slack Incoming Webhook URL SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS
Whether to send Slack even when no findings exist Increase Timeout Increae timeout to 1min 30 sec because we have to scan all the member account so it need to wait time. You can adjust as you like.
Step 6: Create a Slack Incoming Webhook In Slack:
create or choose a channel create an Incoming Webhook copy the webhook URL paste it into the Lambda env variable SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL This allows Lambda to send a summary message to Slack after every run.
Step 7: Deploy and Test the Lambda Manually Create a simple Lambda test event: `{}
Then click Test.
After the run, check these places:
Lambda response CloudWatch Logs S3 bucket Slack channel You should see:
report.json in S3 report.md in S3 Slack summary message These are my security groups that open 0.0.0.0/0 to 3306 and 80. You can also see the ec2 instance name.
Step 8: Add EventBridge Scheduler Right now, manual testing works.= Now automate it.
Go to: Amazon EventBridge -> Schedules → Create schedule
Settings Name: security-audit-daily
Schedule pattern:
rate(1 day)
Target:
Lambda function choose security-audit Execution role for schedule Choose:
Create new role for this schedule This role only needs to invoke Lambda.
Step 9: Verify Automatic Execution Wait for the schedule to trigger the Lambda. ( You can test with 3 min Recurring schdule )
Then verify:
Lambda ran automatically new S3 reports were created Slack received a summary message Final Result At the end of this project, I had a solution that:
scans both management and member accounts detects risky ingress rules focuses only on sensitive ports shows attached resources writes JSON and Markdown reports to S3 sends Slack notifications runs automatically on a schedule Final Thoughts This was one of the best projects I built for learning AWS in a practical way.
It touched a lot of real AWS concepts:
Organizations STS AssumeRole IAM trust and permission policies Lambda execution roles EC2 APIs S3 reporting EventBridge automation Slack integration Most importantly, it helped me move from:
“I know the services”
to
“I can connect them together to solve a real problem”
That is where AWS learning starts to feel real.
summary: Build a centralized AWS security audit workflow that finds risky internet-exposed security group rules across member and management accounts.
title: How to Track Down Risky Security Group Rules Across All of Your AWS Accounts using Lambda

content:
This note demonstrates how to set up a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS, expose it externally, and secure it with HTTPS using AWS Certificate Manager and Route 53.
Prerequisites AWS CLI configured with appropriate permissions eksctl installed kubectl installed and configured Step 1: Create an EKS Cluster First, create a test EKS cluster using eksctl:
eksctl create cluster \ --name test-eks \ --region ap-south-1 \ --version 1.35 \ --instance-types t3.small \ --nodes 1 \ --nodes-min 1 \ --nodes-max 1 This command creates a single-node EKS cluster in the ap-south-1 region.
Step 2: Deploy Sample Applications We’ll deploy two sample applications: a counting service and a dashboard that consumes it.
Counting Service Create counting.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: counting --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: counting spec: ports: - port: 9001 targetPort: 9001 selector: app: counting --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: counting spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: counting template: metadata: labels: app: counting spec: containers: - name: counting image: hashicorp/counting-service:0.0.2 ports: - containerPort: 9001 Apply it:
kubectl apply -f counting.yaml Dashboard Service Create dashboard.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: dashboard --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: labels: app: dashboard name: dashboard spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: dashboard template: metadata: annotations: labels: app: dashboard spec: serviceAccountName: dashboard containers: - name: dashboard image: hashicorp/dashboard-service:0.0.4 ports: - containerPort: 9002 env: - name: COUNTING_SERVICE_URL value: 'http://counting.default.svc.cluster.local:9001' Apply it:
kubectl apply -f dashboard.yaml Step 3: Verify Services Check the services:
kubectl get svc Output:
NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE counting ClusterIP 10.100.149.59 <none> 9001/TCP 7m25s dashboard LoadBalancer 10.100.89.242 a9dd99b117d0c4a35b11a12334f42ac6-409196634.ap-south-1.elb.amazonaws.com 80:30582/TCP 7m24s kubernetes ClusterIP 10.100.0.1 <none> 443/TCP 26m The dashboard service has an external IP provided by AWS Classic Load Balancer.
Step 4: Access via HTTP Initially, the service is accessible via HTTP on port 80.
![[Pasted Image 20260427093556_127.png]]
Step 5: Add DNS with Route 53 Create a CNAME record in Route 53 pointing to the Load Balancer DNS name.
Domain: dashboard.learnops.site
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.webp]]
Step 6: Configure HTTPS Add SSL Certificate Create a public certificate in AWS Certificate Manager for your domain.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-3.webp]]
Update Load Balancer Listener Modify the Load Balancer to use HTTPS on port 443, attaching the certificate.
Before editing:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.webp]]
After adding HTTPS:
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-4.webp]]
Add lobalancer sg to 443 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-6.webp]]
The application is now securely accessible via HTTPS at https://dashboard.learnops.site.
Troubleshooting After configuring HTTPS, you may encounter issues where the dashboard can access the external URL, but the counting service cannot be accessed anymore. This might manifest as a WebSocket error.
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-7.webp]]
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-8.webp]]
ok now ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-9.webp]]
But change back to this ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained.png]]
There is websocket error.
Cloud controller manager do when we change service to loadbalancer. it apply to classic load balancer.
ccm is in eks control plane node but we can’t see it
https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/architecture/cloud-controller/#functions-of-the-ccm
![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-1.png]]
Now we will use with NLB
https://cloud-provider-aws.sigs.k8s.io/service_controller/ you can see in this link about service controller
apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard annotations: service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 ![[Kubernetes Classic Loadbalancer explained-2.png]]
Architecture Diagram Cleanup To clean up:
eksctl delete cluster --name test-eks --region ap-south-1 This removes the EKS cluster and associated resources.
summary: Build a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS.
title: Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS

content:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.
You can expect:
AWS walkthroughs infrastructure and automation notes security-focused troubleshooting practical commands and setup steps Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
summary:
This site is where I document what I learn while working on cloud, DevOps, and security projects.

You can expect:

  • AWS walkthroughs
  • infrastructure and automation notes
  • security-focused troubleshooting
  • practical commands and setup steps

Most posts are written as step-by-step guides so they are easy to follow and revisit later.
title: About

content:
In AWS, we might accidentally leave Security Group rules open to:
0.0.0.0/0.
That means the resource could be reachable from anywhere on the internet. The challenge is not only finding those risky rules, but also understanding the full context in one place:
which account owns the Security Group which Security Group ID it is which EC2 instance or resource is attached to it and which Region it is running in In this project, I built a centralized architecture that can automatically detect these cases across multiple AWS accounts and export a report with all of that information in one setup.
The solution uses AWS Lambda, AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, and EC2 APIs to scan Security Groups, identify attached resources, store reports in Amazon S3, and send a summary to Slack.
When working in a multi-account AWS environment, one common security need is to review Amazon EC2 security groups across all accounts.
In this project, I built an automated solution that:
runs from a central AWS account discovers accounts in AWS Organizations scans security groups across accounts and regions detects risky ingress rules open to 0.0.0.0/0 focuses on sensitive ports such as: 22 (SSH) 3389 (RDP) 3306 (MySQL) writes the results as both: JSON report Markdown report This project helped me understand how to combine AWS Organizations, STS AssumeRole, Lambda, IAM, EC2, and S3 in a real-world security automation workflow.
Project Goal The goal was to scan security groups across multiple AWS accounts and identify risky ingress rules exposed to the internet.
Specifically, I wanted to detect security group rules where:
source is 0.0.0.0/0 port is one of: 22 3389 3306 I also wanted to include both:
member accounts management account Architecture The final solution works like this:
A Lambda function runs in a central AWS account. The Lambda calls AWS Organizations to list all active accounts. For each member account, Lambda uses STS AssumeRole to assume a cross-account role named SecurityAuditRole. For the management account, Lambda scans directly using its own execution role. Lambda calls EC2 APIs to retrieve security groups and their rules. Lambda filters only risky ingress rules. Lambda writes the output to Amazon S3 as: report.json report.md This is IAM Workflow that we will be doing Before You Start You need:
an AWS Organization one management account one or more member accounts permission to create: IAM roles Lambda S3 bucket EventBridge schedule a Slack workspace where you can create an Incoming Webhook Step 1: Create an S3 Bucket for Reports In the management account, create an S3 bucket `security-audit-reports
This bucket will store:
report.json report.md Step 2: Create the Lambda Execution Role in the Management Account Role name `LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole
Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "OrganizationsRead", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "organizations:ListAccounts", "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "AssumeMemberSecurityRole", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": "sts:AssumeRole", "Resource": "arn:aws:iam::*:role/SecurityAuditRole" }, { "Sid": "CloudWatchLogs", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "logs:CreateLogGroup", "logs:CreateLogStream", "logs:PutLogEvents" ], "Resource": "*" }, { "Sid": "WriteReportsToS3", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "s3:PutObject" ], "Resource": "arn:aws:s3:::security-audit-reports-254292659128-us-east-1-an/*" }, { "Sid": "Ec2ReadForManagementAccountScan", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Trust relationships
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "Service": "lambda.amazonaws.com" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } This role is used by the Lambda function.
Step 3: Create the Cross-Account Role in Each Member Account Now switch to each member account and create a role named:
`SecurityAuditRole
This is the role the Lambda will assume using STS.
Trust policy This trust policy allows the Lambda role in the management account to assume it.
{ "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Effect": "Allow", "Principal": { "AWS": "arn:aws:iam::MANAGEMENT-ACCOUNT-ID:role/LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole" }, "Action": "sts:AssumeRole" } ] } Permissions policy { "Version": "2012-10-17", "Statement": [ { "Sid": "ReadEc2SecurityGroups", "Effect": "Allow", "Action": [ "ec2:DescribeSecurityGroups", "ec2:DescribeRegions", "ec2:DescribeNetworkInterfaces" ], "Resource": "*" } ] } Step 4: Create the Lambda Function Go back to the management account.
Create a Lambda function.
Function name: security-audit Runtime: Python 3.x Execution role: LambdaOrgSecurityReviewRole Lambda Code github: <>
Step 5: Add Lambda Environment Variables and Timeout In Lambda, go to:
Configuration → Environment variables
Add these variables:
ROLE_NAME = SecurityAuditRole REPORT_BUCKET = your-s3-bucket-name REPORT_PREFIX = security-group-audit REGION_LIST = us-east-1,eu-west-1 SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL = https://hooks.slack.com/services/xxxx/yyyy/zzzz SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS = false
What they mean ROLE_NAME
Role name to assume in member accounts REPORT_BUCKET
S3 bucket name REPORT_PREFIX
Folder-like prefix inside S3 REGION_LIST
Regions to scan SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL
Slack Incoming Webhook URL SLACK_NOTIFY_ON_NO_FINDINGS
Whether to send Slack even when no findings exist Increase Timeout Increae timeout to 1min 30 sec because we have to scan all the member account so it need to wait time. You can adjust as you like.
Step 6: Create a Slack Incoming Webhook In Slack:
create or choose a channel create an Incoming Webhook copy the webhook URL paste it into the Lambda env variable SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL This allows Lambda to send a summary message to Slack after every run.
Step 7: Deploy and Test the Lambda Manually Create a simple Lambda test event: `{}
Then click Test.
After the run, check these places:
Lambda response CloudWatch Logs S3 bucket Slack channel You should see:
report.json in S3 report.md in S3 Slack summary message These are my security groups that open 0.0.0.0/0 to 3306 and 80. You can also see the ec2 instance name.
Step 8: Add EventBridge Scheduler Right now, manual testing works.= Now automate it.
Go to: Amazon EventBridge -> Schedules → Create schedule
Settings Name: security-audit-daily
Schedule pattern:
rate(1 day)
Target:
Lambda function choose security-audit Execution role for schedule Choose:
Create new role for this schedule This role only needs to invoke Lambda.
Step 9: Verify Automatic Execution Wait for the schedule to trigger the Lambda. ( You can test with 3 min Recurring schdule )
Then verify:
Lambda ran automatically new S3 reports were created Slack received a summary message Final Result At the end of this project, I had a solution that:
scans both management and member accounts detects risky ingress rules focuses only on sensitive ports shows attached resources writes JSON and Markdown reports to S3 sends Slack notifications runs automatically on a schedule Final Thoughts This was one of the best projects I built for learning AWS in a practical way.
It touched a lot of real AWS concepts:
Organizations STS AssumeRole IAM trust and permission policies Lambda execution roles EC2 APIs S3 reporting EventBridge automation Slack integration Most importantly, it helped me move from:
“I know the services”
to
“I can connect them together to solve a real problem”
That is where AWS learning starts to feel real.
summary: Build a centralized AWS security audit workflow that finds risky internet-exposed security group rules across member and management accounts.
title: How to Track Down Risky Security Group Rules Across All of Your AWS Accounts using Lambda

content:
This note demonstrates how to set up a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS, expose it externally, and secure it with HTTPS using AWS Certificate Manager and Route 53.
Prerequisites AWS CLI configured with appropriate permissions eksctl installed kubectl installed and configured Step 1: Create an EKS Cluster First, create a test EKS cluster using eksctl:
eksctl create cluster \ --name test-eks \ --region ap-south-1 \ --version 1.35 \ --instance-types t3.small \ --nodes 1 \ --nodes-min 1 \ --nodes-max 1 This command creates a single-node EKS cluster in the ap-south-1 region.
Step 2: Deploy Sample Applications We’ll deploy two sample applications: a counting service and a dashboard that consumes it.
Counting Service Create counting.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: counting --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: counting spec: ports: - port: 9001 targetPort: 9001 selector: app: counting --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: counting spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: counting template: metadata: labels: app: counting spec: containers: - name: counting image: hashicorp/counting-service:0.0.2 ports: - containerPort: 9001 Apply it:
kubectl apply -f counting.yaml Dashboard Service Create dashboard.yaml:
apiVersion: v1 kind: ServiceAccount metadata: name: dashboard --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 --- apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: labels: app: dashboard name: dashboard spec: replicas: 1 selector: matchLabels: app: dashboard template: metadata: annotations: labels: app: dashboard spec: serviceAccountName: dashboard containers: - name: dashboard image: hashicorp/dashboard-service:0.0.4 ports: - containerPort: 9002 env: - name: COUNTING_SERVICE_URL value: 'http://counting.default.svc.cluster.local:9001' Apply it:
kubectl apply -f dashboard.yaml Step 3: Verify Services Check the services:
kubectl get svc Output:
NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE counting ClusterIP 10.100.149.59 <none> 9001/TCP 7m25s dashboard LoadBalancer 10.100.89.242 a9dd99b117d0c4a35b11a12334f42ac6-409196634.ap-south-1.elb.amazonaws.com 80:30582/TCP 7m24s kubernetes ClusterIP 10.100.0.1 <none> 443/TCP 26m The dashboard service has an external IP provided by AWS Classic Load Balancer.
Step 4: Access via HTTP Initially, the service is accessible via HTTP on port 80.
Step 5: Add DNS with Route 53 Create a CNAME record in Route 53 pointing to the Load Balancer DNS name.
Domain: dashboard.learnops.site
Step 6: Configure HTTPS Add SSL Certificate Create a public certificate in AWS Certificate Manager for your domain.
Update Load Balancer Listener Modify the Load Balancer to use HTTPS on port 443, attaching the certificate.
Before editing:
After adding HTTPS:
Add lobalancer sg to 443 The application is now securely accessible via HTTPS at https://dashboard.learnops.site.
Troubleshooting After configuring HTTPS, you may encounter issues where the dashboard can access the external URL, but the counting service cannot be accessed anymore. This might manifest as a WebSocket error.
ok now But change back to this There is websocket error.
Cloud controller manager do when we change service to loadbalancer. it apply to classic load balancer.
ccm is in eks control plane node but we can’t see it
https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/architecture/cloud-controller/#functions-of-the-ccm
Now we will use with NLB
https://cloud-provider-aws.sigs.k8s.io/service_controller/ you can see in this link about service controller
apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: dashboard annotations: service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: nlb spec: type: LoadBalancer selector: app: dashboard ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 9002 Architecture Diagram Cleanup To clean up:
eksctl delete cluster --name test-eks --region ap-south-1 This removes the EKS cluster and associated resources.
summary: Build a Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS.
title: Kubernetes LoadBalancer service on Amazon EKS
